questions.stem: What is the output of the code segment to the right?
questions.explanation: C int A = 14; int B = 16; out.println(A + B); Prints 30 then carriage return (next line) out.print("A" + "B"); Prints AB but no carriage return out.println('A' + 'B'); Prints 131 (65+66) then carriage return. out.print("A" + B); Prints A16 but no carriage return out.println(A + "B"); Prints 14B

questions.stem: What is output by the code to the right?

choices.C: The code fails to compile when it attempts to resolve the escape sequence "\%".
choices.D: The code fails to compile since unused arguments are passed to the printf() function.
choices.E: The code fails to compile when it attempts to resolve the format conversion of "%%"

questions.explanation: C The string "\\" is the escape sequence to print the character '\', and "%%" is the format for printing the '%' character in formatted strings/outputs. Therefore, "\\%%f" resolves to the string "\%f". Even though the string "%f" appears in the formatted string, note that this does not get filled with a floating-point number – this only happens in the format specifier string, which gets resolved from left-to-right. Some websites might incorrectly make reference to "\%" being an alternative way to print a '%' within a printf statement; however, this has never been an actual feature of Java and will cause a compile-time error since "\%" is not one of the approved escape sequences.
questions.code: out.printf("\\%%f = \%f", 2, 'c');

questions.stem: What is output by the code to the right?
questions.explanation: B The code prints a quote, then 10/4 as 2, then the literal characters \n, then 10/4.0 as 2.5, then two backslashes from \\\\, then a final quote from \", giving "2\n2.5\\"

",A); This prints A formatted with one decimal place (rounded) followed by a carriage return out.print("Five

questions.explanation: D int A = 14; int B = 16; int C = 9; int D = 9; out.print(A % B); Prints 14 out.print(B % C); Prints 7 out.println(A / D); Prints 1 then carriage return (next line) out.print(C / D); Prints 1 out.println(D % C); Prints 0 then carriage return (next line) out.println(A + B / C + D); Prints 24 then carriage return (next line)

questions.stem: How many lines of output are generated by the code to the right? Lines containing only a newline character do not count. The following code produced only one line of output for example. out.println("Hello World");
questions.explanation:
println(s) prints hi and there as lines 1 and 2, respectively (the 
 inside the string is real, and println adds another newline after). printf("%s
%s
", "one", "two
three") produces one (line 3), two (line 4), and three (line 5) since the 
 embedded in "two
three" is also a real newline. print("a

b") gives a (line 6), a blank line (line 7), and b (starting line 8) with no trailing newline. println("x
y") appends x to line 8 making it bx, then y becomes line 9, with println adding a newline. Finally printf("end%s", "
") prints end as line 10 with a trailing newline – giving 10 total.

questions.explanation: C %c will output a character with the ASCII value given. The number before the $ in a printf term denotes which of the following objects will be output by the term, it is 1-indexed.
